description: Named cultivar library — these are the entries users browse in the Library tab and add to their plan. Each entry is a specific, named variety with variety-level growing data. For any missing fields, the app falls back to the matching baseline entry in baseline-cultivars.json (matched by 'crop' field). Generated with AI assistance from general horticultural knowledge; spot-check against seed catalogs or extension sources before relying on specific values.
howToAddACrop: 1. Add at least one named variety here — this is what appears in the Library tab. 2. Ensure a matching baseline entry exists in baseline-cultivars.json (same 'crop' field) for fallback data. If the crop uses non-seed propagation (corms, bulbs, tubers), set 'propagationType' on both entries.

cultivars.notes: Heirloom beefsteak. Large pink fruit, exceptional flavor. Potato-leaf foliage. Needs staking. Prone to cracking.
Heirloom beefsteak. Dusky purple-brown fruit with rich, complex flavor. Excellent slicer.
Hybrid cherry. Orange fruit, intensely sweet. One of the most popular cherry tomatoes. Vigorous, prolific producer. Prone to cracking.
Hybrid slicer. Very early, reliable producer of medium 4-6 oz fruit. Good disease resistance. Widely adapted.
Determinate paste. Classic plum-shaped fruit for sauce and canning. Heavy concentrated set. Verticillium and Fusarium resistant.
Heirloom beefsteak from Crimea. Dark reddish-brown fruit with smoky, salty-sweet flavor. Soft skin, best eaten fresh.
Hybrid beefsteak. AAS winner. Large 10-12 oz fruit with old-fashioned flavor. Strong disease resistance package.
Hybrid slicer. Reliable, heavy-yielding 12-16 oz fruit. VFN resistant. One of the most widely grown home garden tomatoes.
Hybrid determinate. AAS winner. Globe-shaped 7-8 oz fruit. Excellent disease resistance (VFFNTA). Good all-purpose tomato.
Hybrid cherry. Long trusses of very sweet red fruit. Extremely vigorous, can reach 10+ ft. Prolific producer.
Hybrid grape. AAS winner. Elongated 1 oz fruit in clusters. Crack-resistant, good shelf life. Great for snacking and roasting.
Heirloom beefsteak. Very large 1-2 lb pink fruit, meaty with mild flavor. Bred in the 1930s in West Virginia.
Green-and-yellow striped, 3-4 oz fruit. Tangy, zingy flavor. Ripe when yellow stripes deepen and fruit gives slightly.
Heirloom cherry. Pear-shaped yellow fruit, mild sweet flavor. Very vigorous, heavy producer. Popular since the 1800s.
Dark mahogany cherry, rich complex flavor. One of the best-tasting cherry tomatoes. Vigorous, tall plants.
Heirloom paste. Large 8-12 oz oxheart-shaped fruit, meaty with few seeds. Excellent for sauce. Indeterminate, unlike most paste types.
Czech heirloom. Very early, cold-tolerant. Small 2-3 oz red fruit with excellent flavor for an early variety. Potato-leaf foliage. Good for short seasons.
Determinate, bred for Canadian prairies. Very early, compact plants. Medium 6 oz red fruit. Reliable in short, cool seasons.
Classic large red slicer, 1-2 lb fruit. Meaty, solid flesh. Needs strong support. Long season required.
Italian heirloom. Deeply ribbed, fluted fruit. Intensely flavored, acidic. Excellent for sauce and fresh eating.
Cherry/cocktail size. Sweet, well-balanced flavor. Reliable in cool climates. Long trusses of 6-12 fruit.
Red with yellow-orange stripes, 2-4 oz fruit. Tangy flavor. Early and productive. Also sold as Mr. Stripey in some catalogs.
Heirloom. Large heart-shaped pink fruit, 1 lb+. Dense, meaty flesh with few seeds. Good for slicing and sauce.
Semi-determinate. Developed by Rutgers University in the 1930s. All-purpose red 6-8 oz fruit. Good for canning and fresh eating.
Trailing determinate cherry. Compact, cascading habit for hanging baskets and containers. Sweet 1-inch red fruit.
The classic bell pepper. Large, blocky 4-lobed fruit. Green maturing to red. Thick walls, sweet flavor. Open-pollinated.
Hybrid bell. Extra-large, thick-walled fruit. Green to red. Early for a big bell. Good disease resistance.
Long, tapered sweet pepper. Yellow maturing to orange-red. Mild, sweet flavor. Prolific. Good for frying and pickling.
Italian heirloom frying pepper. Long, thin-walled red fruit. Intensely sweet when fried. Slow Food Ark of Taste.
Italian frying pepper. Light green to red, thin walls. Sweet, mild flavor. Prolific producer. Also called Italian frying pepper.
Spanish tapas pepper. Pick small (2 inches) for mild; occasional hot one. Blistered in olive oil with sea salt. Very prolific. Compact plants, good for containers.
Open-pollinated bell. Deep purple skin with green interior. Harvest at purple stage; turns red if left. Compact plants, good for containers.
Classic hot pepper, 30,000-50,000 SHU. Thin 5-6 inch fruit, green to red. Excellent for drying and powder. Prolific. Compact plants, good for containers.
Mexican hot pepper, 10,000-25,000 SHU. Small 2-3 inch fruit, meaty walls. Hotter than jalapeño. Used fresh in salsa and pico. Compact plants, good for containers.
Very hot, 100,000-350,000 SHU. Lantern-shaped orange fruit with fruity, citrusy heat. Long season; start early. Needs consistent warmth.
Mild hot pepper, 500-2,500 SHU. Large 7-8 inch fruit. Good for roasting, stuffing, and green chiles. Also called New Mexico or Hatch.
Mild hot pepper, 1,000-2,000 SHU. Large, heart-shaped dark green fruit. Essential for chiles rellenos. Dried form is ancho.
Medium hot, 5,000-10,000 SHU. Waxy 6-8 inch fruit, yellow to red. Early, productive. Good for pickling and frying.
Very hot, 50,000-100,000 SHU. Small upright 1-inch fruit in clusters. Extremely prolific. Compact plants, good for containers.
Mini sweet peppers in red, orange, and yellow. Snack-size 3 inch fruit, very sweet. Prolific. Compact plants, good for containers.
Early-maturing jalapeño, 2,500-8,000 SHU. Compact plants, good for shorter seasons. Green to red. Open-pollinated.
Japanese small pepper. Thin-walled, wrinkled 3-4 inch fruit. Mostly mild with occasional hot one. Blistered or grilled. Very prolific. Compact plants, good for containers.
APS variety bred for cool climates. Long pods, green to orange to red. 0-500 SHU. Great for powder, salads, pickling.
APS variety. Medium plants, very high yields. Slightly smaller than standard bells, tented shape. Green ripening to bright orange. Very sweet. 0-500 SHU.
APS variety. Thin-walled pods, yellow to orange. 0-500 SHU. Can yield 100+ pods per plant. Dries well for powder. Sweet flavor.

Classic large globe eggplant. Dark purple-black, glossy skin. Harvest at 4-6 in before skin dulls and seeds brown. Needs long warm season — start early indoors. 4-6 fruit per plant. Open-pollinated heirloom.
Japanese slender eggplant, 10-12 in long. Thin skin, few seeds, tender flesh. Earlier and more prolific than globe types. Excellent for stir-fry and grilling. Hybrid.
Asian heirloom mini eggplant. Clusters of slim 3-6 in dark purple fruit. Harvest when finger-sized for best tenderness. Very prolific, few seeds. Good for containers. Open-pollinated.
Most popular mini eggplant. Purple-and-white striped, 2-4 in fruit in clusters. Non-bitter, no peeling needed. Very prolific. Compact plants good for containers. AAS winner 2005. Hybrid.

Ultra-compact mini eggplant (16-20 in). Glossy purple 2-3 in egg-shaped fruit. Earliest eggplant — 45 days from transplant. Bred specifically for containers and small spaces. Thornless. Very prolific. AAS winner 2014. Hybrid.

Green tomatillo for salsa verde. 2-3 in fruit in papery husks. Harvest when husks split. Very prolific — plant at least 2 for cross-pollination. Sprawling plants, benefits from caging. Self-seeds readily.

Purple-skinned tomatillo. Sweeter than green varieties, can be eaten raw. 1-2 in fruit. Turns purple when ripe inside the husk. Plant at least 2 for cross-pollination. Smaller fruit but sweeter flavor.

Husk cherry (Physalis pruinosa). Marble-sized golden fruit in papery husks. Sweet, tropical flavor — pineapple meets tomato. Harvest when husks dry and fruit drops. Very prolific. Polish heirloom. Self-seeds in warm climates.
Indeterminate paste tomato. Classic Italian variety for sauce and canning. Meaty with few seeds.
Indeterminate cherry tomato. Prolific producer of sweet, crack-resistant fruit. Disease resistant.
Trailing/tumbling cherry tomato. Determinate habit, ideal for hanging baskets or containers. Sweet red fruit. Compact habit needs less spacing than standard tomatoes.
Dwarf determinate, 6-12 inches tall. Tiny sweet red cherries. Ideal for windowsills, patio pots, and small containers. No staking needed. Open-pollinated.
Dwarf determinate, 6-12 inches tall. Sweet yellow cherries. Same ultra-compact habit as Red Robin. Windowsill and patio pot size. Open-pollinated.
Dwarf determinate, 6-12 inches tall. Orange cherries, mild sweet flavor. Ultra-compact Robin series. Good for windowsills and small containers. Open-pollinated.
White beet, sweet flavor; cold-tolerant so ok before LSF.
The standard red beet. Deep crimson, smooth globe shape. Sweet and tender. Excellent for canning, roasting, and fresh eating. Reliable heirloom since 1892.
Italian heirloom with red and white concentric rings when sliced. Mild, sweet flavor. Rings fade when cooked, so best raw or lightly roasted. Also called candy cane beet.
Orange-gold roots that don't bleed like red beets. Mild, sweet flavor. Lower germination rate than red varieties — sow extra seed. Greens are especially tender and tasty.
Danish heirloom with cylindrical shape (6-8 in long). Produces uniform slices, ideal for pickling and canning. Dark red, sweet flesh. Higher yield per row foot than globe types.
Round, bright red spring radish. Crisp white flesh, mild flavor. One of the fastest vegetables to harvest. AAS winner 1949. Bolts quickly in heat — sow spring and fall only.
Oblong red radish with white tip. Mild, slightly spicy flavor. Classic French heirloom. Harvest at 2-3 in before they get pithy. Good for succession sowing every 10-14 days.
Mix of red, pink, purple, and white round radishes. Mild flavor, uniform maturity. Fun for kids and market display. Same growing requirements as other spring types.
Large winter radish (3-4 in diameter) with green/white exterior and bright pink interior. Mild, sweet flavor. Sow midsummer for fall harvest. Stores well for months in cold storage.
Japanese daikon, 12-18 in long. Mild, crisp white flesh. Sow midsummer for fall harvest — bolts if spring-sown. Used fresh, pickled, or cooked. Deep loose soil essential.
The classic turnip. Purple shoulders, white bottom, 3-4 in globe. Mild when harvested young. Greens are also edible and nutritious. Sow spring or late summer for fall harvest. Sweetens after frost.

Japanese salad turnip (hakurei type). Small, smooth white globes, 2-3 in. Sweet and crisp enough to eat raw. AAS winner. Harvest young for best flavor. Greens are tender and delicious. Very fast — good for succession sowing.
Large yellow-fleshed root (4-6 in). Sow midsummer for fall harvest. Sweeter than turnips, especially after frost. Excellent storage root — keeps months in cold cellar. Also called swede or neep.

The standard parsnip. White, 10-12 in long. Very slow germination — use fresh seed only, keep soil moist. Sweetens dramatically after hard frost. Can overwinter in ground under mulch. Deep loose soil essential.

Smooth, white parsnip, 10-12 in. Slightly shorter season than Hollow Crown. Fine-grained, sweet flesh. Widely adapted and reliable. Use fresh seed — parsnip seed viability drops sharply after one year.
Reliable early bush bean; do not plant in cold soil.
The standard for green beans. Round, straight 6-inch pods. Stringless. Heavy yields. Good fresh, frozen, or canned. Open-pollinated.
Extra-early bush bean. Round 6-inch pods, good cold tolerance for a bean. Stringless. Good choice for short seasons. Open-pollinated.
Stringless, round dark green 5-inch pods. Reliable and productive. Good all-purpose bush bean. Open-pollinated.
Purple-podded bush bean. Turns green when cooked. Easy to spot for picking. Good cold tolerance. Stringless. Open-pollinated.
Dutch heirloom wax bean. Cream pods with purple streaks. Can be used as snap or shelling bean. Distinctive appearance. Open-pollinated.
Modern hybrid bush bean. Dark green, round 6-7 inch pods. Very productive and uniform. Good disease resistance.
Flat Italian-type bush bean. Broad 5-inch pods, meaty texture. Great for sautéing and braising. Open-pollinated.
Classic heirloom pole bean. Slightly curved 8-9 inch pods. Rich, full bean flavor. Brown-seeded. Needs 6-8 ft trellis. Open-pollinated.
Pole version of the classic. Round, straight 6-inch stringless pods. Heavy producer over a long season. Needs 6-8 ft trellis. Open-pollinated.
Ornamental and edible. Showy red flowers attract hummingbirds. Large flat pods best at 6 inches. Also grown as dried bean. Needs 8+ ft support. Open-pollinated.
Mini romaine; succession-friendly. Shade/row cover in heat; tolerates light frost.
Classic full-size romaine. Tall, upright 10-12 inch heads. Crisp, sweet. Good disease resistance. The standard romaine. Open-pollinated.
Israeli-bred heat-tolerant romaine. Stays sweet and crisp in summer heat. Very slow to bolt. One of the best romaines for warm climates. Open-pollinated.
AAS winner butterhead. Thick, juicy leaves. Compact heads with excellent heat tolerance for a butterhead. Sweet, mild flavor. Open-pollinated.
Miniature butterhead heirloom. Tennis-ball sized heads. Perfect for containers and small spaces. Single-serving size. Open-pollinated.
Red butterhead. Deep red-bronze outer leaves, green and cream interior. Soft, buttery texture. Adds color to salad mix.
Classic loose leaf heirloom. Light green, ruffled, tender leaves. Very fast. Good heat tolerance. Cut-and-come-again. Open-pollinated.
AAS winner red loose leaf. Bronze-red ruffled leaves. Slow to bolt. Never bitter. Good for adding color to salads. Open-pollinated.
Deeply lobed oak-shaped leaves. Mild, sweet flavor. Good heat tolerance. Available in green and red forms. Cut-and-come-again. Open-pollinated.
Summer crisp / Batavian type. Thick, crunchy leaves. Excellent heat tolerance, very slow to bolt. Midway between romaine and iceberg texture. Open-pollinated.
Summer crisp / Batavian type. Large, bright green heads. Very heat tolerant and bolt resistant. Crisp texture holds in warm weather. Open-pollinated.
Smooth leaf, bolt-tolerant. Cold hardy; good for early/late plantings. Bolts above 21C.
Classic savoy-type heirloom. Thick, crinkled dark green leaves. Slow to bolt for a savoy. Excellent flavor. Open-pollinated.
Smooth-leaf hybrid. Very bolt-resistant, good for late spring sowing. Upright habit, easy to harvest and wash. High yield.
Semi-savoy hybrid. Excellent bolt resistance. Dark green, thick leaves. Good disease resistance. Reliable for spring and fall.

Cold-hardy overwintering spinach. Sow in fall for early spring harvest — overwinters as small rosettes under snow or mulch. Large, smooth dark green leaves. One of the hardiest spinach varieties. Also good for early spring sowing.
Chinese broccoli; tender stems/shoots. Prefers cool weather; avoid heat stress.
The classic Italian broccoli. Large blue-green central head, then produces side shoots for extended harvest. Open-pollinated heirloom. Tolerates light frost. Harvest before buds open.
Cold-hardy broccoli bred for fall harvest. Compact heads, prolific side shoot production after main head is cut. Developed at UMass Waltham. Excellent for northern gardens. Open-pollinated.
Classic white cauliflower. Inner leaves curl over the head naturally (self-blanching), keeping curds white. Early maturing, compact plants. More forgiving than most cauliflowers. Open-pollinated.
Vivid purple cauliflower. Color holds when lightly cooked, turns green if overcooked. No blanching needed. Rich in anthocyanins. Mild, slightly nutty flavor. Good for fall harvest.
Early green cabbage. Compact, round 3-4 lb heads. Short core. One of the earliest cabbages to mature. Good for small gardens due to compact size. Heirloom.
Compact red/purple cabbage. Dense, round 2-4 lb heads. Rich in anthocyanins. Good for fresh slaws, braising, and pickling. Stores well. AAS winner. Holds in the field without splitting.

Classic compact Brussels sprout (20-24 in). Produces 50-100 tight sprouts per stalk. Flavor improves dramatically after frost. Start indoors in spring, harvest fall through early winter. Top the plant 3 weeks before final harvest to size up sprouts.
Dwarf heirloom Brussels sprout (20 in). Large 1.5-2 in sprouts. Earlier than Long Island Improved. Developed in the Catskill Mountains. Good choice for shorter seasons. Open-pollinated.

Also called Tuscan, dinosaur, or cavolo nero kale. Dark blue-green strap leaves with bumpy texture. Sweeter and more tender than curly types. Excellent for soups, sautés, and raw salads when massaged. Very cold hardy, flavor improves after frost.

Extremely cold-hardy curly kale. Tightly ruffled blue-green leaves. Survives well below freezing — one of the last crops standing in winter. Tall plants (24-36 in). Hybrid with excellent uniformity. Great for fall/winter harvest.

Flat, oak-shaped leaves with purple stems and veins. Most tender kale variety — excellent raw in salads. Color deepens in cold weather. Napus type (more closely related to rutabaga than other kales). Open-pollinated heirloom.
Compact curly kale (12-15 in). Finely curled blue-green leaves. Good for small gardens and containers. Classic kale flavor. Very cold hardy. Open-pollinated heirloom.

Pale green bulb with crisp, sweet white flesh. Harvest at 2-3 in diameter before it turns woody. Fast-growing. Taste and texture like broccoli stems. Can be eaten raw or cooked. Good for succession sowing spring and fall.

Striking fractal spiral head, chartreuse-green. Nuttier and more delicate than cauliflower. Best for fall harvest — needs cool temps to form heads properly. More finicky than broccoli or cauliflower. Roasting brings out sweetness.
Large purple-red leaves with spicy mustard flavor. Milder when young. Beautiful in salads. Bolts in heat. Cold tolerant. Open-pollinated.
Japanese mustard green. Mild, peppery flavor. Finely serrated feathery leaves. More heat-tolerant than most Asian greens. Cut-and-come-again. Open-pollinated.
Finely cut, lacy chartreuse leaves. Mild for a mustard, good raw in salads. Very ornamental. Fast growing. Open-pollinated.
Full-size hybrid bok choy. White stems, dark green leaves. Bolt-resistant. Upright vase shape. Good for stir-fry and soup.
Compact baby bok choy with pale green stems. Very fast, harvest whole at 6-8 inches. Slow to bolt. Good for containers and succession sowing.
Compact, spoon-shaped green stems (not white). Tender and mild. Can harvest baby or full size. Popular stir-fry variety. Open-pollinated.
Corn salad / lamb's lettuce. Small rosettes, mild nutty flavor. Thrives in cold; best fall through early spring. Germinates poorly above 20°C. Open-pollinated.
Native North American green (Claytonia perfoliata). Distinctive round leaf with stem through center. Mild, succulent. Thrives in cool shade. Self-sows freely.
Heat-loving spinach substitute (Tetragonia tetragonioides). Sprawling habit. Harvest shoot tips. Thrives in summer heat when true spinach bolts. Soak seeds overnight before sowing.
Fast, mild arugula. Broader leaves than wild types. Baby greens in 21 days. Good for succession sowing spring and fall. Bolts in heat. Open-pollinated.
Slow-bolt cultivated arugula. Deeply lobed, oak-leaf shaped. Slightly more heat-tolerant than standard arugula. Good peppery flavor. Open-pollinated.
Perennial wild arugula (Diplotaxis tenuifolia). Narrow, deeply lobed leaves. Intense peppery flavor. Very slow to bolt; harvests all summer. Slower to establish but long-lived. Open-pollinated.

Tropical amaranth green (Amaranthus viridis/dubius). Spinach substitute that thrives in summer heat. Young leaves and tender stems are edible. Staple in Caribbean and West African cooking. Sow after soil is warm.
Sweet, coreless orange carrot. 9-10 inches long. Good for storage. Keep soil moist for germination.
Classic Nantes type. Cylindrical, blunt-tipped, 6-7 in. Sweet, nearly coreless. The standard for fresh eating. Needs loose, well-worked soil.
Nantes type bred for storage. 7-8 in, strong tops for easy pulling. Resistant to alternaria and cercospora leaf blight. Holds sweetness months in cold storage.
Danvers type, 7-8 in with broad shoulders tapering to a point. Tolerates heavier soil than Nantes types. Good for storage and processing. Widely adapted heirloom.
Short, stout Chantenay type (5-6 in). Best choice for heavy or clay soil. Reddish-orange core, sweet flavor. Reliable in less-than-ideal conditions.
Burpless slicing cucumber. Mild flavor, thin skin. Grows 8-10 inches. Needs warm soil to germinate.
The standard slicing cucumber. Dark green, 8-9 inches. Excellent disease resistance (scab, mosaic, mildew). Reliable and productive. Open-pollinated.
AAS winner heirloom slicer. Uniform, straight 8-inch dark green fruit. Good all-purpose cucumber. Vigorous vines. Open-pollinated.
Chinese burpless cucumber. Long, curved 12-15 inch fruit. Crisp, sweet, thin skin. Good heat tolerance. Trellis for straight fruit. Open-pollinated.
Classic pickling cucumber. Short, blocky 5-6 inch fruit. Pick at 2-4 inches for pickles. Heavy producer. Black-spined. Open-pollinated.
Heirloom pickling cucumber since 1880. Smooth, bright green 3-6 inch fruit. Crisp texture, good for dills and bread-and-butters. Vigorous. Open-pollinated.
Hybrid pickling cucumber. Uniform 3-4 inch fruit, concentrated set. Excellent disease resistance (anthracnose, angular leaf spot, scab, mosaic). Very productive.
Compact hybrid bred for containers. Short vines, 8-inch mini cucumbers. Snack-sized, thin skin. Good disease resistance. No trellis needed.

Mexican sour gherkin (Melothria scabra). Grape-sized fruit that looks like tiny watermelons. Citrusy, cucumber-like flavor. Vigorous thin vines, needs trellis or netting. Very prolific. Slow to start but productive once going.
Icebox watermelon, 8-10 lb. Dark green rind, sweet red flesh. Compact vines for small gardens. One of the earliest watermelons — good for short seasons. 2-4 fruit per plant.

Classic striped watermelon, 20-25 lb. Light green with dark green stripes. Very sweet, bright red flesh. Vigorous vines need lots of space. Disease resistant. 2-3 fruit per plant. Needs long warm season.
Classic muskmelon, 3-5 lb. Heavily netted rind, deep orange flesh. Very sweet and aromatic. Ripe when stem slips easily from fruit. Tolerates heat well. Open-pollinated heirloom. 3-5 fruit per plant.

Personal-sized muskmelon, 4 in diameter. Extra-sweet golden flesh. Earliest cantaloupe — bred at University of Minnesota for short seasons. Compact vines (3-4 ft). Perfect for northern gardens and containers.

Smooth white rind, sweet pale green flesh. Needs long warm season (110 days). Ripe when blossom end gives slightly and skin turns creamy. Transplant only in northern gardens. 2-3 fruit per plant. Stores better than cantaloupe.
Classic dark green zucchini. Very prolific. Pick at 6-8 inches for best flavor. Compact bush habit. Open-pollinated.
Italian heirloom ribbed zucchini. Nutty flavor, dense flesh. Best at 6-8 inches. Gorgeous flowers for stuffing. Open-pollinated.
Bright yellow zucchini. Same culture as green types. Easy to spot for harvest. Mild, slightly sweeter flavor. Open-pollinated.
Classic yellow summer squash with curved neck. Best picked small (4-6 inches). Bumpy skin, buttery flavor. Very prolific. Open-pollinated.
Scallop / pattypan type. Light green, flat disc-shaped fruit. Pick at 3-4 inches across. Tender, nutty flavor. Bush habit. Open-pollinated.
The classic butternut. Tan, bell-shaped 3-5 lb fruit. Sweet, orange flesh. Stores 3-6 months. Vining habit, needs space. Open-pollinated.
Small oblong striped squash. Edible skin, no peeling needed. Sweet, creamy flesh. Shorter storage (2-3 months). Semi-vining, more compact. Open-pollinated.
Classic acorn squash. Dark green ribbed 1-2 lb fruit. Mild, slightly sweet. Good for baking halved. Stores 2-3 months. Semi-bush habit. Open-pollinated.
Flesh separates into noodle-like strands when cooked. Yellow oblong 3-5 lb fruit. Stores 2-3 months. Vining habit. Open-pollinated.
Japanese kabocha type. Bright red-orange teardrop shape, 3-5 lbs. Sweet, chestnut-like flavor. Thin edible skin. Stores 3-4 months. Open-pollinated.
Small 5-8 lb pie pumpkin. Sweet, smooth flesh ideal for baking. Much better flavor than jack-o-lantern types. Vining habit. Open-pollinated.
Long-day yellow storage onion. Start seeds early indoors. Harvest when tops fall over. Cures well.
Red-skinned shallot with pink flesh. Strong flavor, stores well. Bolt-resistant and vigorous.

Long-storing yellow shallot. Copper skin, pale yellow flesh. Mild, sweet flavor. Excellent keeper — stores 10-12 months. Seed-grown, produces single large bulbs rather than clusters. Good bolt resistance.
French heirloom banana shallot. Elongated torpedo shape, pink-brown skin, rosy flesh. Rich, complex flavor prized in French cooking. Also called cuisse de poulet (chicken leg). Stores 6-8 months.
Famous sweet onion from Washington state. Large, mild bulbs up to 1 lb. Long-day variety. Poor storage — use within 1-2 months of harvest. Best eaten fresh or lightly cooked.
Deep red long-day storage onion. Globe-shaped, firm, with strong flavor. Stores 4-6 months. Good disease resistance. One of the best red onions for northern gardens.

Popular hardneck garlic. Large bulbs with 4-7 large cloves. Strong, rich flavor. Plant cloves in fall (6-8 weeks before ground freezes). Harvest midsummer when lower leaves brown. Produces edible scapes in spring. Stores 6-9 months.

Softneck garlic with mild, rich flavor. Large bulbs with 10-20 cloves. Artichoke type — layers of cloves. Can be braided for storage. Stores 9-12 months. More cloves per bulb than hardneck. Originally from Inchelium, Washington.

Softneck garlic planted in early spring as soon as soil can be worked. Produces smaller bulbs than fall-planted garlic due to lack of cold vernalization. Milder flavor. Harvest midsummer when lower leaves yellow. Stores 3-5 months. Use softneck varieties for best spring results.
Early summer leek. Tall, slender shafts with long white stems. Fastest leek to mature. Mild, delicate flavor. Not as cold-hardy as winter types — harvest before hard frost.
Very hardy overwintering leek. Short, thick blue-green shafts. Survives to Zone 5 with mulch. Harvest late winter through early spring. Strong flavor, holds well in ground without bolting.
Classic bunching onion. Non-bulbing, forms clumps. Very cold hardy — can overwinter in many climates. Succession sow every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest. Mild flavor, harvest at any size.
Red-stemmed bunching onion. Vivid crimson color from base partway up the stalk. Mild, sweet flavor. Non-bulbing. Color intensifies in cool weather. Attractive in salads and as garnish.
Shell pea, early maturing. Cold tolerant, sow as soon as soil can be worked. Provide support for climbing.
Semi-dwarf shelling pea (24-28 in). Large pods with 8-11 peas each. Resistant to fusarium wilt and downy mildew. Reliable heavy yields.
Also called Homesteader. Heritage variety prized for sweet flavor. More heat-tolerant than most peas. Vines 24-30 in. Widely adapted.
The original snap pea. Tall vine (5-6 ft), needs sturdy trellis. Sweet, thick edible pods. AAS winner 1979. Eat pods whole or shell when mature.
Dwarf snap pea (24-30 in). No trellis needed. Earliest snap pea variety. AAS winner 1984. Good for containers and small gardens.
Improved Sugar Snap with powdery mildew resistance. Tall vine (5-6 ft). Thicker walls and plumper pods than the original. Heavy yields.
Compact snow pea (28 in). Resistant to pea enation mosaic virus and powdery mildew. Most popular snow pea variety. Flat, sweet pods.
Tall snow pea vine (4-5 ft), needs trellis. Large 4-5 in pods. Productive heirloom. Tender and sweet when harvested young before peas swell.
Perennial; harvest begins year 3. All-male hybrid bred in Ontario. Very cold hardy (Zone 3). High yields, excellent spear quality.
June-bearing; developed in PEI. Large firm berries, excellent flavor. Very cold hardy (Zone 3). Remove flowers first year to establish plants.

Perennial green (Chenopodium bonus-henricus). Young shoots eaten like asparagus in spring, leaves like spinach. Light harvest year 1, full harvest from year 2. Cold hardy to Zone 3. Slow to establish but long-lived.

Perennial herb/vegetable (Levisticum officinale). Intense celery flavor. Grows 4-6 ft tall. Leaves, stems, and seeds all edible. Very cold hardy (Zone 3). One plant is usually enough. Long-lived once established.

Vigorous perennial grown from root cuttings. Harvest roots in fall after frost for best pungency. Very cold hardy (Zone 3). Spreads aggressively — plant in a contained area or large pot. One or two plants is plenty for most households.

The most widely grown rhubarb. Green stalks blushing pink-red. Can be grown from seed (unlike most rhubarb). Harvest stalks only — leaves are toxic. Do not harvest first year. Very cold hardy (Zone 3), needs winter chill. Productive for 10+ years.

Deep red stalks that hold color when cooked. Sweeter and less tart than Victoria. Grown from crown divisions only, not seed. Harvest stalks only — leaves are toxic. Do not harvest first year. Very cold hardy (Zone 3), needs winter chill. Productive for 10+ years.
Cut-and-come-again flowers. Pinch for bushier growth. Heat lover.
Semi-double to fully double flowers. Tolerates poor soil. Self-sows readily.
Classic Italian large-leaf basil, the standard for pesto. Pinch flower buds to prolong leaf production. Blackens at the slightest frost.
Sturdy purple stems with a distinctive anise-licorice flavor. More heat-tolerant than Italian types and slower to bolt in hot weather.
AAS winner with deep purple foliage. Dual-purpose ornamental and culinary basil. Slightly slower to mature than green varieties; germination can be less uniform.
Compact plant with bright citrus aroma and flavor. Excellent in teas, seafood, and desserts. Smaller leaves than Genovese.
Spicy-sweet cinnamon aroma with attractive purple stems and pink flower spikes. Good in fruit dishes, teas, and Asian cuisine.
Slow-bolt variety with good leaf production. Bolts in heat; best as a cool-season crop. Successive sow every 2-3 weeks. Seeds (coriander) harvestable at 90-100 days.
One of the slowest-bolting cilantro varieties available. Vigorous, bushy growth with heavy leaf yields. Best in cool weather; sow in early spring or fall.
Flat-leaf type with large dark leaves and robust flavor preferred for cooking. Biennial that bolts in second year. Slow to germinate; soaking seed overnight helps.
Tightly curled leaves, classic garnish variety with mild flavor. Biennial. Cold-tolerant and slow to bolt. Slow to germinate; soaking seed overnight helps.

Root parsley with long, straight white roots (5-8 inches). Nutty parsley-celery flavor, excellent in soups and stews. Leafy tops edible but stronger and tougher than leaf parsley. Cold hardy, withstands light frost. Very slow germination — keep soil moist. Direct sow only (taproot). Stores several months in cool conditions.
Compact variety (2-3 ft) that produces seed heads early, ideal for pickling. Dill does not transplant well due to its taproot. Successive sow for continuous leaf harvest.
AAS winner. Dwarf variety (18 in) ideal for containers. Slow to bolt with prolific, finely cut foliage. Better for leaf harvest than seed production.
Classic culinary thyme with warm, earthy flavor. Woody subshrub growing 6-12 inches tall; reliably winter-hardy to Zone 5.
Citrus-scented thyme with a bright lemon flavor, excellent for fish and poultry. Low spreading habit; hardy to Zone 5.
Considered the best culinary oregano with the strongest, most pungent flavor. Spreads by runners; cut back before flowering to maintain flavor.
Milder, sweeter flavor than Greek oregano; well-suited to tomato sauces and Italian cooking. Hardy to Zone 5.
Classic culinary sage with large gray-green aromatic leaves. Woody subshrub; very cold-hardy to Zone 4. Replace plants every 3-4 years as they become woody.
Ornamental and culinary sage with striking purple-tinged leaves. Flavor is similar to common sage; hardy to Zone 4.
Most cold-hardy rosemary cultivar, surviving to -23C (Zone 6). Light blue flowers; open, slightly lax habit. Typically propagated from cuttings.
Tall, upright rosemary growing to 6 feet with strong resinous flavor and dark blue flowers. Less cold-hardy than Arp; reliable only to Zone 8. Typically propagated from cuttings.
Tender perennial grown as an annual. Milder and sweeter than oregano with a delicate floral quality. Frost-sensitive; bring indoors or treat as annual in cold climates.
Classic annual bean herb with a warm, peppery flavor. Direct sow after last frost; does not transplant well due to a delicate root system.
Stronger and more pungent than summer savory, with a resinous, almost pine-like bite. Woody evergreen subshrub; cold-hardy to Zone 5.
Classic spearmint (Mentha spicata) with sweet, mild mint flavor. Vigorous spreader via runners; grow in containers or with root barriers to prevent takeover. Very cold hardy (Zone 3-8).
Sterile hybrid (Mentha x piperita) with high menthol content; stronger and more pungent than spearmint. Propagate by division or cuttings only. Aggressive spreader; contain in pots.
Mild onion-flavored leaves (Allium schoenoprasum). Very cold hardy (Zone 3-9) and one of the first herbs up in spring. Attractive purple flowers are edible. Clump-forming; divide every 3-4 years.

Flat-leaved garlic chives (Allium tuberosum), also called Chinese chives. Mild garlic flavor used in Asian cuisine. White star-shaped flowers in late summer. Self-seeds freely; deadhead to control spread.
Lemon-scented member of the mint family (Melissa officinalis). Vigorous grower that self-seeds prolifically; deadhead or contain to prevent spread. Excellent for teas. Hardy to Zone 4.

True French tarragon (Artemisia dracunculus var. sativa) with distinctive anise-licorice flavor essential to French cuisine. Does not produce viable seed; propagate by division or cuttings only. Hardy to Zone 4.

Delicate, anise-flavored annual in the parsley family (Anthriscus cerefolium). Prefers cool weather and partial shade; bolts quickly in heat. Direct sow in early spring or fall. Essential in fines herbes.
Annual tea herb (Matricaria chamomilla) with apple-scented daisy-like flowers. Grows 2-3 ft tall. Direct sow on soil surface; seeds need light to germinate. Self-seeds reliably.

Tangy, lemony-flavored perennial green (Rumex acetosa) excellent in salads, soups, and sauces. Very cold hardy (Zone 3-9) and one of the earliest spring greens. Remove flower stalks to prolong leaf harvest.

Japanese herb (Perilla frutescens var. crispa) with complex basil-mint-cinnamon flavor, essential in sushi and Asian cuisine. Grows about 2 ft tall. Needs warm soil to germinate; sow after last frost.

Tropical grass (Cymbopogon citratus) grown for its lemon-flavored stalk bases used in Thai and Vietnamese cooking. Grows 3-5 ft tall. Propagate by division; hardy only in Zone 9-11, grown as annual elsewhere.

Natural sweetener herb (Stevia rebaudiana) with leaves 30-40 times sweeter than sugar. Hardy only in Zone 8-11; grown as annual elsewhere. Seed germination is very poor; transplants or cuttings strongly preferred.
AAS winner with multicolored stems in red, orange, yellow, pink, and white. Ornamental and productive. Cut-and-come-again harvest of outer leaves. More heat-tolerant than spinach.
Classic white-stemmed heirloom with thick, crunchy ribs and large dark green savoyed leaves. Very productive and heat-tolerant. The standard chard for decades.
Also sold as Rhubarb Chard. Deep crimson stems with dark green leaves. Attractive in both garden and kitchen. Slightly earthier flavor than white-stemmed types.
Striking red-and-white striped stems with green leaves. Very ornamental; slightly milder flavor than solid-colored varieties. Grows vigorously to 18-24 inches.
Gold-fleshed, buttery flavor. The most popular yellow potato in North America. Good for roasting, mashing, and boiling. Moderate storage life. Developed at University of Guelph.
Red-skinned, white-fleshed all-purpose potato. Thin skin, excellent for boiling and potato salad. High yield and widely adapted. Short storage life; best eaten fresh.
Large white-fleshed all-purpose potato. Excellent for frying, baking, and mashing. Very high yields. Good disease resistance. Moderate storage life.
Very early red-skinned potato developed in North Dakota. Smooth, oblong tubers with white flesh. Excellent boiled or roasted as new potatoes. Short storage life but one of the earliest to harvest.

The classic baking potato. Large, oblong, russeted skin with dry, fluffy white flesh. Best for baking, frying, and mashing. Excellent storage life (2-3 months). Needs consistent moisture; prone to hollow heart if watering is erratic.
Bicolor (se) with white and yellow kernels. Sweet, tender, and popular with home gardeners. Plant in blocks of at least 4 rows for good pollination, not single rows.
Classic open-pollinated heirloom from 1902. Yellow kernels with old-fashioned corn flavor. Short stalks (5 ft), good for smaller gardens. Plant in blocks for pollination.
White (su+) with large 8-9 inch ears. Considered by many the best-tasting white corn. Late season; needs a long growing season. Tall stalks (8 ft). Plant in blocks for pollination.

The most widely grown sweet potato in North America. Orange flesh, uniform shape, high yields. Grown from slips (rooted sprouts), not seed. Needs 100+ frost-free days and warm soil. Cure after harvest for best storage.
Developed at NC State. Orange flesh with excellent sweetness and smooth texture. Good disease resistance. Produces uniform, compact roots. Grown from slips. Becoming the commercial standard.
Red-skinned with orange flesh. One of the earliest sweet potatoes (90 days), making it viable in shorter-season northern gardens. Grown from slips. Moist, sweet flesh.

Classic heirloom collard with large blue-green leaves. Very heat-tolerant for a brassica; grows well through southern summers. Flavor improves after frost. Harvest lower leaves for continuous production.
Compact, non-heading variety with dark green leaves. More bolt-resistant than Georgia Southern. Good cold tolerance; overwinters in mild climates (Zone 7+). Harvest lower leaves continuously.
Compact hybrid with uniform dark green leaves. Faster to mature than heirloom types (60 days). Good bolt resistance and cold tolerance. Tender texture with mild, sweet flavor.

AAS winner and the standard okra variety. Spineless pods, harvest at 3-4 inches for best tenderness. Thrives in heat; struggles below 15C. Soak seed overnight to speed germination. Plants grow 4-5 ft tall.
AAS winner with striking deep red pods that turn green when cooked. Ornamental and productive. Slightly shorter plants (3-4 ft) than Clemson Spineless. Harvest pods young at 3-4 inches.

The standard garden celery. Tall, dark green stalks with good flavor. Long indoor start (10-12 weeks) required. Needs consistent moisture and cool temps; bolts in heat. Slow germination; surface sow, seeds need light.
Compact, earlier-maturing celery with smooth, glossy stalks. Better bolt resistance than Tall Utah. Self-blanching habit produces milder, less stringy stalks.

Celery root with smooth, round bulbs and strong celery flavor. Excellent roasted, in soups, or as remoulade. Very long season (10+ weeks indoor start). Cold-tolerant; harvest after light frost for best flavor. Stores well.

Hybrid Florence fennel with thick, rounded white bulbs and anise flavor. Good bolt resistance. Harvest bulb at ground level when 3-4 inches across. Fronds also edible. Bolts in heat or with transplant shock.
Open-pollinated Florence fennel, slightly earlier than Orion. Flat, broad bulbs with mild anise flavor. Best sown midsummer for fall harvest to avoid bolting. Fronds edible as herb.

Produces many small purple florets over weeks rather than a single head. Flavor is sweeter and more tender than heading broccoli, especially after frost. Can be grown as an overwintering crop in mild climates.

Earlier-maturing selection of purple sprouting broccoli, ready about 10 days before the standard type. Compact plants. Harvest shoots when florets are tight; cut regularly to encourage more side shoots.

The original broccolini (broccoli x gai lan hybrid). Tender, asparagus-like stems with small florets and a mild, sweet flavor. Cut main head first to encourage prolific side shoots. Less cold-hardy than sprouting broccoli.

Classic cutting flower. Plant corms 10-15 cm deep, pointy side up, after last frost. Stagger plantings every 2 weeks for continuous blooms. Tall varieties (90-120 cm) need staking. Dig up corms before first fall frost in cold climates. 70-90 days to first bloom.

Vigorous trailing pansy, spreads 60-75 cm. Excellent for containers, hanging baskets, and window boxes. More heat-tolerant than standard pansies but still prefers cool weather. Blooms spring through early summer, and again in fall. Self-cleaning — minimal deadheading needed.

Spreading petunia, trails 75-100 cm. Great for containers, hanging baskets, and ground cover. Blooms continuously until frost without deadheading. Feed regularly — heavy feeders in containers. More mounding than Wave, easier to manage in mixed containers.

Double azalea-type blooms on tall 60-90 cm stems. Excellent cut flower. Surface sow — seeds need light to germinate. Pinch at 4-6 leaves for branching. Thrives in cool spring/fall weather; may stall in summer heat. Tolerates light frost.

Tall single-stem variety, 75-90 cm. One of the best snaps for cutting — strong straight stems. Does not require pinching. Surface sow. Tolerates light frost. Classic snap jaw flowers in a bright color mix.

Dramatic trailing rope-like burgundy flower tassels up to 60 cm long. Plants reach 90-120 cm. Drought-tolerant once established. Direct sow preferred. Edible leaves and seeds. Makes a striking dried flower. 65-75 days to first bloom.

Classic white sweet alyssum, 10-15 cm tall. Honey-scented groundcover. Blooms prolifically spring to frost. Tolerates light frost. Attracts beneficial insects. Self-sows. Shear back mid-summer to encourage rebloom.

Deep violet-purple sweet alyssum, 10-15 cm tall. Compact spreading habit. Honey-scented. Tolerates light frost. Excellent edging plant and companion for vegetables — attracts hoverflies and other beneficial insects.
